chapters.two-tool-architecture: How Rediacc connects to your server

events.text: Register the server from the command line. You give it a name, its IP address, and the SSH user. The name is just a local alias. We use machine-11 so it matches the IP, but it can be anything you like, such as prod-db or web-1. There is no separate init step; the config file is created automatically on first use.
events.afterText: Server registered. Host keys were scanned automatically.
events.prose: Register the server with a name, IP address, and SSH user. The name is just a local alias (we use machine-11 to match the IP, but any label works, like prod-db or web-1). No init step is needed; the config file is created automatically on first use, and rdc falls back to your standard ~/.ssh key.

events.text: Next we provision the server. This installs renet and creates the encrypted datastore.
events.afterText: Done. Renet is installed and the datastore is ready.
events.prose: Provision the server. This installs renet and creates the encrypted datastore.

events.text: Here is the file that holds everything: your machines, the SSH key, and infrastructure settings. It is plain JSON, created automatically. You can keep several configs, one per environment such as production or staging, and switch between them with the config flag.
events.afterText: One portable file. Copy it to another laptop to drive the same servers.
events.prose: Inspect the JSON file that holds it all: machines, SSH key, and infrastructure. You can keep several configs, one per environment, and switch with the --config flag. See the multiple configs guide linked below.

narrations.text: Alright! Let's add your first server. Two commands and you're set.
There are two pieces. The command-line tool runs on your laptop, and an orchestrator called renet runs on the server. The CLI talks to it over SSH, so you never need to log into the server manually.
And that's a wrap on adding a server! See you in the next one.
